title: Assisting a stuck lamb at lambing
subtitle: Recognising malpresentation and correcting it before the ewe or lamb is exhausted.
excerpt: Most ewes deliver unaided. When they do not, the delay between a ewe that is straining without progress and a keeper who acts can make the difference between a live lamb and a dead one. This tutorial covers how long to wait, the normal presentation to feel for, and the three most common malpresentations with their corrections.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Lambing (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk
SAC Consulting, Lambing management guidance. https://www.sac.co.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A lamb positioned in the uterus in a way that prevents normal delivery. Common malpresentations include head-back (both legs forward but head turned to the side), leg-back (one or both front legs folded at the knee), and breech (hindquarters first with legs forward).
The correct lambing position: front hooves first, soles down, with the head resting on both front legs. In this position the widest parts of the lamb pass through the pelvis in sequence.

The strong involuntary uterine contractions that follow the first stage of labour. Once a ewe is actively straining without delivering, the clock starts: thirty minutes of active straining without progress is the standard intervention point for a small keeper.

content.text: The standard intervention point for a small keeper is thirty minutes of

with no lamb showing. A ewe that is restless, pawing, and looking at her flanks but not yet in strong contractions may be in the first stage of labour; leave her alone. A ewe straining hard with nothing at the vulva after thirty minutes needs investigation.

attrs.title: Normal presentation
attrs.body: In normal presentation, the front hooves appear first, soles down, with the head resting on the cannons (the lower portion of the front legs). You should be able to feel the tongue of the lamb just inside the vulva if one or two hooves are visible. The head follows, then the shoulders, which are the widest point.

content.text: Wash your hands and arms to the elbow with soap and water. Apply lubricant liberally. Approach the ewe from behind. Have someone hold her head or work her against a corner. If you are not familiar with the anatomy, have your vet's number ready before you start and call early rather than late.
Insert one hand gently and assess what you can feel. In
, you feel both front hooves and the head just behind them. The three most common
are: head-back (both front legs forward but the head turned back to one side), one or both legs folded at the knee, and breech (tail first with the hind legs forward into the ewe's belly).

entries.symptom: Head turned back: two hooves visible but no head
entries.cause: The head has turned sideways in the uterus, usually to the right
entries.fix: Push the lamb slightly back to make room, cup the head in your palm, and guide it forward until the nose rests on the front legs. Assist with the next contraction once the head is in position.

entries.symptom: One or both knees folded (knee joint presents first instead of hoof)
entries.cause: One or both front legs folded at the knee, so the knee rather than the hoof presents
entries.fix: Push back gently, cup the hoof, and bring the leg forward to its full extension before pulling.

entries.symptom: Tail or hindquarters visible with no legs (breech)
entries.cause: Lamb is reversing out, hind legs tucked forward into the uterus
entries.fix: This is the most difficult: the hindquarters must pass through the pelvis without the legs to push against. Get veterinary help if you are not experienced with breech deliveries.

content.text: Clear mucus from the lamb's nostrils immediately. Spray the navel with strong iodine within the first hour to prevent joint ill entering through the cord. Check the ewe for a second lamb: twins are common in most lowland breeds. Record the ewe tag, lamb tag, birth weight, and any assistance given.
